tier1_posts.text:
@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I know this is weird, but whether something is speech for First Amendment purposes does not depend on what it is but on what it is doing. If, and to the extent that, it is part of the process of humans exchanging thoughts and emotions, it's speech.

If I "distribute a Bible" by

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I know this is weird, but whether something is speech for First Amendment purposes does not depend on what it is but on what it is doing. If, and to the extent that, it is part of the process of humans exchanging thoughts and emotions, it's speech.

If I "distribute a Bible" by
@benrayfield You don't need any of the rights the licence grants you to use the software, only to distribute it or create derivative works.

@Eve777M Close, it's a mango daiquiri.

It reminds me of the smoky daiquiris that I used to make for my father. He'd ask me what kind of daiquiri it was and I'd say, "It's a hickory daiquiri, Doc!".

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Yes. In the Microsoft anti-trust case. In various ADA website cases. Also, it's just obvious from how 1A law works. If "code is speech" works, then "burning cloth is not speech" would have worked in Texas v. Johnson.
@GloriousEggroll The law doesn't put any restrictions at all on how the indication can be changed? Because if it does, that's verification.

@DietCoke_Esq Suppose you have to argue that your case isn't moot in front of the Supreme Court because you really want to set precedent. That clause lets you argue that you have not gotten all the relief the lower court could have given you no matter what the other side concedes or agrees to.
@pcfreak30 @GWHayduke97 @jimmy_wales How are they different in a way that's relevant to the argument that you can't compel code changes because code is speech?

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Exactly. That's why this argument is nonsense and courts reject it. Compelling functional changes in a functional code product is not like compelling expression in an argument or work of art for 1A purposes.

@peirce_alva @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I don't see why any of that would have any bearing on the argument that compelling code is compelling speech, the very argument courts consistently reject when the government demands functional changes to a functional product.
@peirce_alva @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne What does that have to do with whether the government is compelling code and therefore compelling speech?
@MicahAlexKenda1 @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne How so? They're both functional products protected by copyright and the government is arguing the law demands functional changes.

@jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Isn't this the same argument Microsoft made, and every US court soundly rejected, when the government argued they had to make modifications to Windows to remove anti-competitive aspects such as bundling?

probability.last_reasoning: Bear case increased from 10% to 12% due to Hormuz escalation (oil shock potential), extreme fear at 10, and USD/JPY deepening past 157. If $100 oil materializes, it triggers the full Japan stress feedback loop and could cascade to CRITICAL compound stress. Base case narrowed slightly from 40% to 42% as institutional momentum (Kraken Fed access, Morgan Stanley ETF, White House engagement) provides floor. Mid case reduced slightly from 38% to 35% reflecting increased macro uncertainty. Bull case trimmed from 12% to 11% — the geopolitical environment is less conducive to exponential adoption acceleration.

bear_case.score_reasoning: Elevated from prior assessment. The Hormuz escalation adds genuine macro tail risk that could suppress institutional risk appetite for 2-3 quarters. ODL data opacity remains the single largest evidentiary gap. RLUSD's Ethereum concentration undermines the XRPL-specific utility argument. Permissioned DEX at zero adoption after 12 days is early but not encouraging.

competing_infrastructure.name: SWIFT GPI
competing_infrastructure.status_text: SWIFT continues incremental upgrades serving 11,500+ banks. No dramatic improvement but network effects remain formidable — institutional inertia is SWIFT's greatest asset.

competing_infrastructure.name: Visa B2B Connect
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Visa's blockchain-based B2B settlement network expanding quietly. Direct competition for the same cross-border corridors XRPL targets.

competing_infrastructure.name: JPMorgan Kinexys
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Kinexys (formerly Onyx) processes significant daily volumes on a private chain. JPMorgan actively fighting stablecoin regulatory framework per today's Dimon pushback — they want to own settlement, not cede it.

competing_infrastructure.name: BIS Project Nexus
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Multi-CBDC bridge project represents sovereign alternative to private settlement tokens. Kill switch 9 (Competitive Displacement) remains active — no evidence Nexus has chosen to operate independently of XRPL, but no evidence of integration either.

competing_infrastructure.name: Ethereum Institutional
competing_infrastructure.status_text: RLUSD's own data shows ~78% of supply on Ethereum ($1.24B) vs XRPL ($345M). If Ripple's own stablecoin prefers Ethereum for institutional deployment, this undermines the XRPL-as-settlement-layer narrative directly.

bear_case.odl_stagnation: No ODL volume data for 12+ months. The thesis's central utility mechanism remains completely unverifiable. Ripple's decision to stop publishing this data is itself a bearish signal that hasn't been adequately explained.
bear_case.token_velocity_concern: Token velocity data unavailable (Kill Switch 8: UNKNOWN). Without hold-time data, we cannot assess whether XRP functions as a settlement asset or a speculative pass-through — this distinction is fundamental to price thesis.

macro_headwinds.name: Global Recession Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Hormuz closure → $100 oil → global stagflation risk. Fear & Greed at 10. Dubai crashing. Energy shock is classically recessionary and would freeze institutional adoption timelines.

macro_headwinds.name: Crypto Winter Signals
macro_headwinds.status_text: XRP at $1.40 is well off highs. Extreme Fear at 10. However, BTC ETF inflows ($1.45B/week) and institutional milestones (Kraken Fed access) argue against full winter — this looks like a macro-driven correction, not a structural unwind.

macro_headwinds.name: Regulatory Reversal Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Executive branch actively defending crypto policy. White House engagement with Coinbase and Ripple. No signals of reversal. Risk is stagnation (Clarity Act stuck in pending), not reversal.

macro_headwinds.name: Rate Hike Extension
macro_headwinds.status_text: US 10Y at 4.05% stable. If oil shock materializes, Fed faces inflation/recession dilemma. Rate cuts may be delayed or reversed, tightening liquidity for risk assets including crypto.

bear_case.bear_narrative: HOLD POSITION. Bear pressure 34/100 — moderate, driven almost entirely by one structural vulnerability: three years without verifiable ODL settlement data, with the Q3 2026 transparency kill switch now six months away. Everything else resolved favorably: Hormuz is a risk, not a crisis (oil at $81 says so); CBDC interop is real but years from production; 7 of 10 Layer 3 null hypotheses held, meaning most headline threats don't survive scrutiny. The core paradox: XRP shows relative strength (+3.55% during Extreme Fear at 10) with genuine institutional momentum (7 ETFs, $1.45B BTC weekly inflows, Coinbase at the White House) — yet the primary use case that justifies the entire thesis remains a black box. The thesis is holding, but it's holding on institutional credibility and regulatory momentum, not on verified settlement utility. That distinction matters, and the clock is running.
